title: Is the market held up by too few stocks?
question: How narrow is the S&P 500 really, and does narrow, top-heavy leadership actually warn that a broad drawdown is coming when people take profits in the winners?
finding: The 'few stocks hold it up' half is true and stark; the 'so a crash is coming' half is not. Concentration is extreme: top-10 names = 43.9% of cap, effective-N 38.8 (the index trades like ~39 equal-weight stocks), and the top-10 supplied 64.7% of the trailing-12-month return while the median member returned +8.7% vs the index's +22.3%. But the predictive claim fails a falsification test on a survivorship-clean, point-in-time S&P 500 (membership 2016-2026, ~500 names/day): when under half the members were above their 200-day average the next 12 months returned +22.5% median / 89% positive, and under 30% it was +25.8% / 100% - narrow breadth preceded ABOVE-average returns, not drops. As a drawdown alarm it was worse than the base rate (10%+ fall within a year 35% of the time after narrow breadth vs 41% unconditional). The classic index-high-with-fading-breadth divergence (+13.6% fwd, base rate) and pure concentration (+15.3% fwd, 93% positive) also showed no timing edge; the null survives EMA, a 30-60% threshold sweep, and an adversarial steelman. The market is two-speed, not sick: AI chips + AI-power/electrification (GEV +95%, VRT +175%, MYRG +160%) lead while software/consumer/fintech and merchant power-gen (IPPs, 0% above 200-day) lag. The one unconfirmed lead is the user's actual mechanism - narrow breadth AND the top-15 leaders breaking their own 200-day - which tripled the next-quarter 10%+ drawdown odds (40% vs 14%) but rests on only ~10 independent windows and misses the significance bar. Verdict: concentration Yes; median-stock-lag Yes; crash-timing No/Conditional - fragility and a thin cushion, not a datable signal. Clean history begins 2016 (owned data cannot cleanly reach the 2000/2008 crash casualties), so the leaders-break lead awaits a delisted-inclusive deep-history follow-up.
